title: The bureaucracy
description: Practice your skills in this exercise on the federal bureaucracy.

assessment_items.question_content:
Congress passes a law reducing the amount of violence that children can be exposed to on television.

**Which of the following actions can the Federal Communications Commission (FCC) take to implement this measure?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Write guidelines about how much violence can be depicted during times children are likely to be watching TV
Refuse to air violent TV shows on their broadcast networks
Issue an executive order banning the production of violent TV shows
Lobby Congress to decrease federal funding for TV networks that broadcast violent TV shows

assessment_items.question_content:
The Environmental Protection Agency issues a fine to a factory that is not complying with federal regulations on the maximum number of pollutants it can release into the atmosphere. 

**This is an example of which of the federal bureaucracy’s responsibilities?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Enforcement
Rulemaking
Testifying before Congress
Congressional oversight

assessment_items.question_content:
Examine the table and answer the question below. 

|| **Percentage of workers by highest level of education attained** ||
Degree earned | Private sector employees| Federal employees
- | - | -
Bachelor's | 21.9% | 33.4%
Master's | 6.8% | 15%
Professional or doctorate | 2.7% | 7%
Source: [The Heritage Foundation](https://www.heritage.org/jobs-and-labor/report/inflated-federal-pay-how-americans-are-overtaxed-overpay-the-civil-service)

**Based on the data shown in the table, which of the following statements is true?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Federal employees are more likely than private sector employees to have master’s degrees
It is necessary to have a bachelor’s degree to become a federal employee
Private sector employees are more likely to have master’s degrees than bachelor’s degrees
More federal employees have master’s degrees than bachelor’s degrees

assessment_items.question_content:
The House Committee on Veterans Affairs, the Department of Veterans Affairs, and the VFW (Veterans of Foreign Wars) work together to achieve policies that benefit veterans.

**This is an example of which of the following?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Iron triangles
Issue networks
Corporate lobbies
Political action committees

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following is the best example of an issue network?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Government and private groups opposed to a proposal to run a pipeline through Native American land
A national organization committed to influencing legislation to promote small businesses
A Congressional subcommittee on issues facing senior citizens
An independent executive agency regulating telecommunications

assessment_items.question_content:
| Iron triangles | Issue networks
- | - | -
**A** | groups of experts including people inside and outside of the government  | close-knit coalitions of the bureaucracy, interest groups, and Congress
**B** | focus on a broad policy area | focus on a specific policy issue 
**C** | larger and more complex than issue networks | more open and fluid than iron triangles
**D**| influence policy formulation | influence election campaigns

**Which of the following is an accurate comparison of iron triangles and issue networks?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Comparison A
Comparison B
Comparison C
Comparison D

assessment_items.question_content:
| Iron triangles | Issue networks
- | - | -
**A** | operate over short periods of time to address a specific policy issue | operate over long periods of time to address a policy area
**B** | influence the enforcement of laws | influence public policy goals 
**C** | alliance made up of the bureaucracy, interest groups, and Congress | large, complex, and fluid webs of influence
**D** | disappear and change over time | appear when policies they care about reach Congress

**Which of the following is an accurate comparison of iron triangles and issue networks?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Comparison A
Comparison B
Comparison C
Comparison D

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following statements best explains the difference between iron triangles and issue networks?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Issue networks are more likely to focus on a single issue, whereas iron triangles focus on a policy area.
Issue networks are stable relationships that last for long periods of time, whereas iron triangles appear when a particular policy issue becomes popular.
Issue networks only include members of Congress, bureaucrats, and interest groups, whereas iron triangles include members of the media and other experts.
Issue networks rely on the bureaucracy to implement policy goals, whereas iron triangles rely on grassroots organizations.

assessment_items.question_content:
After a hard-fought election, President Greene appoints a major campaign donor as ambassador to Greece. 

**This is an example of which of the following concepts?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Patronage
Civil service
Corruption
Specialization

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following is a consequence of the merit system in the bureaucracy?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Greater professionalism in the bureaucracy
Greater inefficiency in the bureaucracy
Greater fragmentation in the bureaucracy
Greater loyalty to the president’s party in the bureaucracy

assessment_items.question_content:
Examine the cartoon and answer the question below. 

![Political cartoon depicting Andrew Jackson, who championed granting offices to party supporters, riding a pig. The caption reads "To the victors belong the spoils."](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/c18c27765d59541dd06e140b0de4c16d.jpg)

*Political cartoon depicting Andrew Jackson, who championed granting offices to party supporters, riding a pig.*

**Which of the following best describes the message in the political cartoon?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The patronage system leads to corruption
The president should reward political allies with government jobs
Civil service reform is unnecessary
The bureaucracy is slow and inefficient

assessment_items.question_content:
President Dwight Schrute has several positions to fill within the federal bureaucracy. 

**Which of the following is an example of filling a position based on merit?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: promoting the Deputy Attorney General to Attorney General after four years of strong service
nominating a member of his political party as the secretary of energy after she supported him during the campaign
asking a high-level campaign fund-raiser to serve as ambassador to Germany
choosing a member of the National Security Council that would appease members from the opposition party

assessment_items.question_content:
President Meredith Palmer has several positions to fill within the federal bureaucracy. 

**Which of the following is an example of filling a position based on political patronage?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: nominating a member of her political party as the secretary of state after he offered political support during the campaign
asking the former head of the Environmental Protection Agency to serve as chair of the Council on Environmental Quality
choosing a secretary of commerce that would appease members from the opposition party
requesting the director of the Central Intelligence Agency (CIA) to stay in her position even though the previous administration appointed her

assessment_items.question_content:
Congress passes the Clean Power Expansion Act, requiring a 25 percent reduction in greenhouse gas emissions from power plants over the next ten years. The Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) holds public hearings with [[☃ definition 1]], including industry representatives and environmental groups. Based on this input, the EPA issues detailed rules outlining specific emission limits, procedures for monitoring emissions, and deadlines for different types of power plants.

**Which core function of the federal bureaucracy is illustrated in this scenario?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.definition: people or groups who have an interest in or are affected by a project, decision, or organization

choices.content: Developing regulations that clarify how the law will be implemented
Issuing fines to companies that fail to meet federal emissions targets
Collaborating with Congress to draft amendments to environmental legislation
Advising the president on international climate policy negotiations

assessment_items.question_content:
**What is an accurate comparison of iron triangles and issue networks?**

|   | **Iron Triangles** | **Issue Networks** |
| - | - | - |
| **A** | Are informal and short-lived policy coalitions | Are formal and permanent parts of government |
| **B** | Rely on courts and media as key participants  | Rely mainly on lobbyists and bureaucrats  |
| **C** |  Feature long-term relationships among agencies, committees, and interest groups | Involve temporary alliances focused on specific policy issues |
| **D** | Appear in primarily long-term economic policymaking and agenda-setting contexts | Appear only in short-term social policymaking contexts |


[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Comparison A
Comparison B
Comparison C
Comparison D

assessment_items.question_content:
**Why do federal agencies regularly testify before congressional committees during the implementation of federal policy?**


[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: To help Congress carry out its oversight authority and evaluate how laws are enforced
To generate public support for their regulations and improve their agency’s reputation
To formally request additional funding from Congress for future policy priorities
To express concern about and challenge judicial decisions that limit agency power
